ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Cha Ca La Vong. FOODON_03302051 (fish dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for this Vietnamese turmeric-marinated grilled catfish preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for this specific Vietnamese dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual ethnic dish preparations at this level of specificity.

serving.description: 1 plate (fish portion with vermicelli and accompaniments)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Cha Ca La Vong is approximately 400 g, comprising a portion of grilled turmeric catfish served in a hot pan with rice vermicelli, fresh dill, spring onions, roasted peanuts, and homemade shrimp paste on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Catfish, channel, cooked, dry heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with turmeric marinade and vermicelli component estimates from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Cha Ca La Vong is a composite dish. Core fish nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for channel catfish cooked dry heat (FDC ID 175177). Carbohydrate and fibre contributions from rice vermicelli are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for rice noodles. Sodium is elevated to reflect fish sauce and shrimp paste marinade. Values represent a blended per-100g estimate of the complete dish as served (fish, noodles, herbs, peanuts, condiments in typical proportions). This is an estimated composite; exact values will vary by restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles (vermicelli) GI reference — University of Sydney GI Database; composite dish GI estimated from primary carbohydrate source
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cha Ca La Vong has no direct GI measurement in the literature. The dominant glycemic carbohydrate source in this dish is rice vermicelli. Rice vermicelli GI is reported at approximately 52 (low GI) in the University of Sydney GI Database. The overall dish GI is estimated at 52 based on this primary carbohydrate source, moderated by the high protein (catfish) and fat (peanuts) content which further attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as: (52 x 48 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 25.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from fish sauce and shrimp paste marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce and shrimp paste, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 1920 mg per standard 400 g serving) approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli; mitigated by high protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic index of 52 (low) and glycemic load of approximately 25 per serving make this dish moderately suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control of the vermicelli component is advised, and pairing with the high-protein catfish and peanuts helps blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from catfish offset by high sodium and peanut fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Catfish provides beneficial polyunsaturated fatty acids including omega-3s which support cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content from fish sauce and shrimp paste poses a risk for individuals with cardiovascular disease; consumption should be moderated and accompanied by a request for reduced condiment use.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cha Ca La Vong (Chả Cá Lã Vọng) is one of Vietnam's most celebrated and iconic dishes, originating from Hanoi and named after the legendary La Vong restaurant on Hang Son Street that has served this dish for over a century. The dish features wild-caught crystal-eye catfish (ca lang or ca anh vu) that is marinated in a fragrant blend of turmeric, galangal or ginger, shrimp paste, fish sauce, and spring onions, then lightly grilled over charcoal to develop a golden crust. At the table, the fish is finished in a sizzling hot pan with generous amounts of fresh dill and spring onions, creating an aromatic, herbaceous presentation unique in Vietnamese cuisine. It is served alongside rice vermicelli noodles (bun), roasted peanuts, fresh herbs, and a side of mam tom (fermented shrimp paste) or nuoc cham dipping sauce. The flavour profile is complex: earthy turmeric, fragrant dill, savoury fish sauce, and the nuttiness of peanuts combine with the delicate, slightly sweet flesh of the catfish. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of lean protein (approximately 12.5 g per 100 g), omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, B vitamins including B12, and selenium. The primary clinical concern is the high sodium content from fish sauce and shrimp paste, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low) based on the rice vermicelli component, making it moderately suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when the noodle portion is controlled. In Singapore, this dish is served at Vietnamese specialty restaurants such as Co Chung Restaurant and represents an authentic Hanoi culinary tradition transplanted to the Singaporean dining scene.

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for Cha Ca La Vong exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy catfish data combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA rice noodle data and adjusted for the composite dish composition. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database rice vermicelli entry. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy catfish source entry and is not available from FAO/INFOODS SEA for this dish composition. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for dry-heat cooked catfish with no added hydrogenated fats. Nutrient values represent a blended per-100g estimate of the complete dish as plated (fish approximately 45%, vermicelli approximately 30%, herbs and peanuts approximately 15%, condiments approximately 10% by weight). Sodium is elevated above plain catfish values to reflect fish sauce and shrimp paste contributions typical of this preparation. The dish is classified under Singapore cuisine in the input source because it is served at a Singapore restaurant; however, the dish origin is Vietnamese (Hanoi) and has been reclassified accordingly. The input food_name contained promotional text 'EXCLUSIVE DISH' which has been removed to produce the canonical name. GI category is low (GI = 52, which is less than or equal to 55 per Veritas threshold rules).
